beats.activity: Wake up, coffee, and morning routine at home
beats.reasoning: Ryan starts the day at home in the U-District (near his listed address). After yesterday's high energy and late evening with records, he's taking a slower morning—energy at 40/100 suggests he'll be more deliberate today. Steady rain outside keeps him inside longer.

beats.activity: Commute to Tukwila for first guitar lessons
beats.reasoning: Ryan heads to his Tukwila workplace for teaching. The SR-520 eastbound closure (9am–noon) doesn't affect him going south, but he leaves early enough to avoid any secondary congestion ripple. Steady rain is manageable for a commute.

Three hours of back-to-back lessons (typically 30–45 min each with short breaks). This is his bread-and-butter work. Rain doesn't affect indoor teaching. SR-520 closure is over by noon, so it won't impact his departure.

beats.activity: Quick lunch and rest in Tukwila
beats.reasoning: Instead of venturing out in the drizzle, Ryan grabs a casual lunch near his teaching space—maybe a deli or café he knows. Rain is still steady (won't clear until 2pm), so staying put makes sense. Low energy means he's not seeking adventure today.

Rain is still active at 1pm, but Ryan heads home for the afternoon. He's not the type to linger downtown in grey weather, and his low energy suggests he'll want downtime. Takes the I-5 corridor northbound.

beats.activity: Browse Half Price Books and pick up a few used vinyl records
beats.reasoning: Rain is clearing by 2pm. Ryan can't resist a bookstore—it's his stated weakness. He ducks into the Capitol Hill branch (a short drive or bus ride from the U-District) for an hour and a half of aimless browsing and vinyl digging. This scratches his thrifting itch and matches his mood: indulgent but low-key.

beats.activity: Walk through nearby neighborhood and head home
beats.reasoning: After the bookstore, Ryan takes a slow walk through Capitol Hill to shake off the rain and clear his head. The weather is improving, and he's still in low-energy mode—no big plans, just moving gently through the afternoon. Heads back toward the U-District.
Ryan arrives home mid-afternoon as the rain fully clears. He's got a couple of hours before evening, and his low energy means he'll stay put now.

beats.activity: Prep for evening lessons and practice guitar
beats.reasoning: Ryan uses the late afternoon to review lesson plans for any students he might have this evening, and noodle around on his own guitars. This is typical downtime for an independent music teacher—mixing work prep with personal play.

beats.activity: Dinner at home; leftovers and a simple meal
beats.reasoning: Ryan cooks or reheats a simple dinner at home. Low energy and rainy day mean no restaurant trip. He's winding down for the evening, staying cozy indoors.

beats.activity: Evening leisure: reading, listening to records, maybe some light social texting
beats.reasoning: Ryan spends the evening at home with a book, the records he picked up earlier, and quiet reflection. He texts a close friend or two but stays in. This low-key evening is a natural wind-down after a modest day and matches his introverted, close-friend-focused personality.

Ryan heads to bed at 11pm, getting a solid 7.5 hours of sleep. The overnight beat ends at 6:30am on June 3, visibly crossing midnight and giving him restorative rest after a low-energy, rain-dampened day.

diary: Woke up to that familiar grey Seattle drizzle—the kind that makes you want to stay under the covers with your coffee. The SR-520 closure had everyone scrambling this morning, but I'd timed the Tukwila run early enough to dodge the worst of it. My students were solid today; there's something about a rainy Tuesday that makes people actually focus on their fingering instead of rushing through. Grabbed a quick sandwich after, then headed back through Capitol Hill on the way home. Half Price Books called to me like it always does—picked up a couple of used vinyl records that were too good to pass up. The neighborhood felt quiet and contemplative in that drizzle. Back home by afternoon, I ran through some new material and prepped for tonight's lessons. Dinner was just leftovers, but the evening felt restorative somehow. Put on one of my finds, let the rain do its thing against the windows. These slower days remind me why I love this life.
thought_process: The rain and bridge closure shaped my whole day—I was grateful I'd built in early commute time, which kept me from the traffic stress that probably hit others. Teaching in that weather felt meaningful; my students were present and engaged, less distracted than usual. I chose to linger at Half Price Books because I needed that tactile, unhurried moment—something grounding after the morning's logistical scramble. The walk through Capitol Hill felt like I was reclaiming some agency after being shuttled around by traffic and schedules. By evening, I'd shifted into a reflective gear. The new records and quiet time at home felt earned, restorative. Some days you're just riding the schedule; today I found pockets of intention within it.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
